title: Fractals of Simple Random Walks in Two Dimensions: A Monte Carlo Study
authors: Jiang Zhou, Ziru Deng, Pengcheng Hou
abstract: We present a Monte Carlo study of the fractal geometry of clusters formed by discrete-time simple random walks (sRW) of $L^2$ steps on a periodic square $L\times L$ lattice. We verify with high precision that the asymptotic behavior of the cluster mass follows $M/L^2 \simeq (\ln L)^{-1} [\fracπ{2}+b (\ln L)^{-2}]$, with $b\approx -(π/2)^{-2}$, demonstrating marginal ``logarithmic fractals". We further determine the fractal dimension of the hull to be $d_{\rm hull}=1.333\,29(14)=4/3$, in excellent agreement with the prediction of Schramm-Loewner evolution ($\rm SLE_{8/3}$) for the Brownian frontier universality class. More importantly, we analyze the chemical distance $S$ spanning the cluster and obtain strong evidence that it asymptotically scales as $S\sim L(\ln L)^{1/4}$, lying exactly on the theoretical upper bound for the chemical distance for level-set percolation clusters on the two-dimensional Gaussian free field. Our numerical results show that the sRW cluster exhibits a conformally invariant external frontier and contains highly efficient asymptotically linear connective paths.
summary: This paper provides a high-precision Monte Carlo study of the fractal properties of 2D simple random walk clusters. It confirms that these clusters exhibit 'logarithmic fractal' mass scaling and a hull dimension of 4/3, placing them in the Brownian frontier universality class. Notably, the study finds that the shortest path (chemical distance) within the cluster scales as L(ln L)^(1/4), supporting the sharpness of theoretical upper bounds. The results also clarify how periodic boundary conditions affect the approach to asymptotic scaling.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the fractal geometry and scaling properties of clusters formed by 2D simple random walks, specifically focusing on mass scaling, hull dimension, and chemical distance.
main_result: The authors confirm the mass scaling follows a logarithmic fractal behavior, determine the hull dimension to be 4/3 (consistent with SLE 8/3), and find evidence that the chemical distance scales as L(ln L)^(1/4), matching the Ding-Wirth upper bound.
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations on periodic square lattices up to size L = 2^16, using nonlinear least-squares fits, finite-size scaling analysis, and gap-function analysis.
model_or_system: A discrete-time simple random walk (sRW) on a 2D periodic square lattice (discrete torus) with a fixed number of steps N = L^2.
key_observables: Cluster mass (M), hull perimeter (L_hull), and spanning chemical distance (S).
important_parameters: Lattice size L, number of steps N = L^2, and periodic boundary conditions.
main_assumptions: The argument regarding the sharpness of the chemical distance bound via unwrapping the torus to an infinite plane is heuristic.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a typical sRW trajectory; Figure 2 displays mass scaling via semi-log plots; Figure 3 shows data collapse for mass corrections; Figure 4 shows hull perimeter scaling; Figure 5 shows the collapse of the chemical distance scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of sRW fractal geometry, describes the Monte Carlo methodology, presents detailed scaling results for mass, hull, and chemical distance, and concludes with discussions on boundary conditions and open questions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Typical sRW trajectory of L2 steps on a periodic L × L square lattice with L = 800, started from the red point (0, 0). The trace is weakly space filling and leaves holes on all length scales.
FIG. 2. Rescaled cluster mass L2/M versus ln L. The large- L data approach a straight line of slope 2/π, consistent with M ∼(π/2)L2/ ln L. The inset shows the approach of aL to π/2.
FIG. 4. Plot of ln L versus ln L. The asymptotic linear behav- ior is consistent with L ∼L4/3. Inset: collapse of the rescaled hull perimeter L/L4/3 versus L−1.
FIG. 3. Collapse of aL versus (ln L)−2. The asymptotic lin- earity provides direct graphical support for w = 2. Inset: test of (π/2−aL)−1/2 ∝ln L, consistent with the fitted correction amplitude.

FIG. 5. Finite-size scaling of the chemical distance S. (a) Collapse of s4 ≡(S/L)4 versus ln L. (b) Gap function ∆s4(L) versus ln L. The gap function shows neither clear signs of asymptotic convergence nor evidence of divergence within the accessible range of system sizes.
